content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural delay of the selection age (Finnish/Polish model)'. Hungary's early-selection system at ages 10–12 increases achievement inequality and socio-economic stratification without raising average performance, and similar learning gains to Portugal's are achievable through non-structural reforms (curriculum clarity, assessment transparency, retention reduction, targeted support) within a comprehensive framework.
opportunities: Potential achievement gains in the range of Poland's observed 0.16-0.28 SD across subjects, with the largest gains for at-risk/vocational-track-bound students, if Hungary's context proves sufficiently comparable.

Reduced intergenerational income elasticity, by analogy with Finland's observed 23% reduction (0.30 to 0.23) following its delay of tracking from 11 to 16, though Finland's effect was bundled with welfare-state expansion and teacher-education reform not isolated from the tracking-age effect itself.
strengths: Portugal maintained a comprehensive (non-selective) school structure through compulsory basic education and improved PISA results by approximately 31 points (0.3 standard deviations) between 2000 and 2015–2018 without introducing early tracking.

Portugal's documented gains in learning outcomes resulted from curriculum standards clarification, increased school autonomy, regular student assessment, and vocational pathway development—not from changes to selection age.
threats: The scenario identifies a substantial reversal risk citing the Polish precedent (reform reversed after 18 years despite documented gains), and the only entrenchment device contemplated (a cross-party political pact) is not specified with statutory safeguards, independent monitoring triggers, or supermajority requirements, leaving the reform structurally exposed in the same way Poland's was.

Fidesz's immediate framing of any 'unified model' as totalitarian ('no example since communism since') establishes this scenario as the highest-visibility target for culture-war opposition among all five, given it most closely matches the minister's initially floated (then retracted) unified four-year model idea.
weaknesses: Whether targeted support (TEIP-type) can close achievement gaps rather than merely reducing dropout rates remains uncertain because no longitudinal study has tracked student competency trajectories beyond retention reduction.

The fiscal and pedagogical cost of phase-out or structural reform of Hungary's 8-year grammar school track (replacing it with comprehensive entry and specialist bands at 14+) has not been modeled in published Hungarian research.
